coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress is a growing concern, necessitating more effective antioxidants for complex environments. Herein, we developed a composite nanozyme, DVI-CDs-Cu, with exceptional antioxidant and antibacterial properties by utilizing DVI-CDs derived from Chinese herbs: Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root, complexed with Cu2+ ions. The DVI-CDs with abundant surface functional groups exhibited excellent superoxide dismutase-like (SOD-like) activity while forming stable complexes with copper ions. The attached copper ions performed the peroxidase-like (POD-like) function and formed a cascade effect, greatly enhancing the antioxidant capacity of the DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme and strengthening its antibacterial property. The nanozyme was able to comprehensively clear 90 % of reactive oxygen species (ROS) while killing approximately 98 % of bacteria. The potential application of the nanozyme as a ROS scavenger was demonstrated by exploring its application to cigarette filters, capable of eliminating approximately 80 % of ROS. This provides a new case for the design and development of CDs-metal ions synergistic bionano-enzymes.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled copper herbal carbon dots nanosheets with powerful antibacterial properties: A robust cascade antioxidant nanozyme for scavenging reactive oxygen species

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress is a growing concern, necessitating more effective antioxidants for complex environments. Herein, we developed a composite nanozyme, DVI-CDs-Cu, with exceptional antioxidant and antibacterial properties by utilizing DVI-CDs derived from Chinese herbs: Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root, complexed with Cu2+ ions. The DVI-CDs with abundant surface functional groups exhibited excellent superoxide dismutase-like (SOD-like) activity while forming stable complexes with copper ions. The attached copper ions performed the peroxidase-like (POD-like) function and formed a cascade effect, greatly enhancing the antioxidant capacity of the DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme and strengthening its antibacterial property. The nanozyme was able to comprehensively clear 90 % of reactive oxygen species (ROS) while killing approximately 98 % of bacteria. The potential application of the nanozyme as a ROS scavenger was demonstrated by exploring its application to cigarette filters, capable of eliminating approximately 80 % of ROS. This provides a new case for the design and development of CDs-metal ions synergistic bionano-enzymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous advancement of biological and clinical medicine, there has been a profound exploration into the factors contributing to damage in the human body (Bazopoulou et al., 2019; Rodriguez-Nuevo et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including superoxide anion (•O2 –), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), are highly reactive molecules with unpaired electrons that can be generated during normal metabolic processes in living organisms, playing a crucial role in sustaining vital physiological functions (Eccleston, 2023; Fu et al., 2023). However, under abnormal conditions leading to their excessive accumulation within the body, oxidative stress occurs, resulting in aberrant cell death and triggering various diseases (Cheung and Vousden, 2022; Liu et al. 2022a, b; Wang et al., 2022). Modern medical research has identified ROS imbalance as a significant contributor to cell death observed in iron-related disorders, alcoholic liver injury, and inflammatory diseases (He et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2023; Roca et al., 2022; Shu et al., 2023). Furthermore, escalating levels of ROS generated by photochemical reactions and environmental air pollutants from increasing atmospheric pollution may also exert detrimental effects on human health (He et al., 2022; He and He, 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2017). Therefore, it is imperative to search for efficient and environmentally friendly free radical scavengers to safeguard human well-being.

Conventional antioxidants, such as ascorbic acid, exhibit limited efficacy in neutralizing free radicals due to their low bioavailability, instability, and dose dependency (Li et al., 2023). In recent years, nanozymes have garnered significant attention as a novel class of antioxidants. These include nanoparticles of noble metals such as Au, Ag, and Pt (He et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2018; Zhang et al. 2023a, b), nano-oxides of Ce, Mn, and Co (Dong et al., 2014; Yao et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020), and nanostructures of organic substances such as glutathione (Liu et al. 2022a, b). These nanozyme mimetic systems demonstrate efficient catalytic activity similar to natural enzymes while overcoming the stability and preparation challenges encountered by conventional enzymes (Zandieh and Liu, 2023). Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are amorphous carbon particles with characteristic sizes of less than 10 nm (Du et al., 2021). Notably, metal ion-doped CDs offer enhanced catalytic performance through facilitated charge transfer and substrate adsorption at the metal–carbon interface. Moreover, their exceptional biocompatibility and cost-effectiveness render them highly versatile for diverse applications (Gao et al. 2023a, b; Kim et al., 2016; Li et al., 2022; Xue et al., 2021).

Herbs, as a valuable natural resource, are extensively utilized in traditional medicine and herbal therapies due to their abundance of bioactive compounds (Bennetzen, 2019; Li et al., 2018; Woo et al., 2012), some of which possess properties for scavenging free radicals. Moreover, the utilization of herbs for the preparation of therapeutically beneficial CDs has been reported (Li et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2020). Copper is a prevalent constituent of natural antioxidant enzymes, and its copper-catalyzed cross-coupling mechanism involving Cu+/Cu2+ redox cycling can be efficiently conducted in the presence of free radicals (Beletskaya and Cheprakov, 2004; Luo et al., 2023). This has inspired the development of numerous nanozymes based on copper nanostructures that exhibit remarkable antibacterial properties and enzyme-catalyzed activities (Lin et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2022; Xu et al. 2023a, b). The abundant functional groups and large specific surface area on CDs' surfaces offer the potential for metal ion adsorption.

Herein, drawing inspiration from natural copper-containing antioxidant enzymes and existing studies on CDs nanozymes, we prepared a composite nanozyme of DVI-CDs-Cu with highly efficient antioxidant and antibacterial properties (Scheme 1 ). Polyphenolic compounds such as chlorogenic acid are mainly found in Dandelion (Liu et al., 2021), while Violet contains a variety of alkaloids (Dong et al., 2018), and alkaloids such as indirubin are predominant in Isatis root (Liu et al., 2018). DVI-CDs were derived from carbon precursors found in Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root herbs to mimic the carbon-containing molecular structure of natural enzymes that have demonstrated remarkable scavenging abilities towards •O2 – and •OH. To further enhance its antioxidant capacity, we generated a cascade catalytic effect by constructing a carbon–metal interface of CDs-Cu through simple coordination complexation. Copper nanostructures were grown via adsorption onto the abundant surface functional groups of DVI-CDs to act as metal ions within the enzyme-like structure. It improves the yield of the carbon–metal interface over the in-situ preparation process using directly doped carbon dots with metal ions and avoids the waste of by-products due to the self-generation of oxides or hydroxides from metal ions under hydrothermal conditions. The superoxide dismutase-like (SOD-like) activity of CDs effectively dismutates •O2 – into H2O2 and O2, while the peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity of copper catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 to produce •OH and the generated •OH can then be efficiently scavenged by CDs. Considering the large number of smokers worldwide and the fact that cigarette combustion produces high levels of ROS, which increases exposure risk to environmental ROS, we applied DVI-CDs-Cu to cigarette filtration systems to simulate and evaluate their ability to scavenge ROS from the environment.

Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root were procured from the local Chinese medicine market and originated from Changbai Mountain, Hebei, and Gansu in China respectively. Anhydrous copper sulfate (CuSO4, AR) was purchased from LingFeng Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). L-Methionine (98 %) was obtained from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Riboflavin (98 %) was acquired from Macklin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT, 99 %) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4, AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99 %), phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) were provided by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Terephthalic acid (TA, 99 %) was provided by D&B Biological Science and Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were used without further purification. Deionized water (18.2 MPa) was obtained from an ultrapure water system for laboratory purposes.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken using a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by a MiniFlex-600 (Rigaku, Japan) scanned at 5° min−1 in the 2θ range from 10° to 90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 were collected using a Nicolet-5700 (Thermo Nicolet, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples was tested using a SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). The fluorescence spectra of the samples were tested using FS5 (Edinburgh, UK) and the UV absorption spectra of the samples were tested using UV-3600 UV–VIS-NIR SPECTROPHOTOMETER (Shimadzu, Japan). JPB-607A Portable Dissolved Oxygen Meter (Leici, China) was used to test the dissolved oxygen concentration. All digital photographs were taken with an iPhone-11 (Apple, USA).

The DVI-CDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root (100 mg each) were pulverized and transferred into a 50 mL beaker. Then, 20 mL of deionized water and 2 mL of 1 M NaOH were added to the beaker and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor with a PTFE liner (50 mL capacity) and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered through a nylon filter membrane (0.22 μm) and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove any residue. The carbon quantum dots solution was obtained by concentrating the filtrate using rotary evaporation, followed by dialysis treatment in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain DVI-CDs powder, which was stored in the refrigerator for future use.

The pH of a mixture containing 15 mL 10 % DVI-CDs solution and 1 mL 200 mg mL−1 CuSO4 solution was adjusted to 7.4 by using 1 M NaOH solution, transferred to a round-bottomed flask, and stirred at 55 °C for 3 h. After several centrifugations and washings with deionized water, the resulting products were freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed with Gaussian 16 C01 (Frisch et al., 2016). B3LYP (Becke, 1993; Stephens et al., 1994; Vosko et al., 1980) functional was used. Grimme’s dispersion correction (Grimme et al., 2010) with Becke-Johnson damping (Grimme et al., 2011) was applied. The def-TZVP basis set (Schäfer et al., 1994) was used on the Cu atom and Pople’s 6-31G* basis set was used on the other atoms. Solvation model based on density (SMD) (Marenich et al., 2009) was applied in the geometric optimizations and energy calculations. Wavefunction analyses were performed with Multiwfn 3.8 (dev) (Lu and Chen, 2012). The ESP evaluation code based on LIBRETA library (Zhang and Lu, 2021) was utilized.

L-methionine and riboflavin were used to generate •O2 – while NBT was utilized to monitor its quantity. Typically, a mixture of L-methionine (6 mM), riboflavin (10 μM), and NBT (25 μM) was added to PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) along with varying concentrations of the sample (0–30 μ L-1). The mixed solutions were subjected to irradiation using a 10 W LED daylight lamp to generate •O2 – during the experiment, and the absorbance at 560 nm of these mixed solutions was measured every 60 s by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The scavenging efficiency of •O2 – was calculated as Eq. (1).

Where A0 is the absorbance of NBT, and A1 and A2 are the absorbance at 560 nm resulting from the reduction of NBT in the absence and presence of nanozyme, respectively. To investigate thermal stability, DVI-CDs-Cu samples were incubated at temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 80 °C for 1h.

A Fenton reaction based on Fe2+/H2O2 was used to produce •OH and TMB was used to detect the amount of •OH. Specifically, TMB color developer (10 mM) was added to a PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing H2O2 (10 mM) and FeSO4 (10 mM) with different concentrations of the sample (0–50 μg mL−1). The absorbance of the mixed solution at 652 nm was measured every 60 s by UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The scavenging efficiency of •OH was calculated by Eq. (2).

Where A0 is the absorbance of TMB, and A1 and A2 are the absorbance at 652 nm produced by oxTMB oxidized from TMB in the absence and presence of nanozyme, respectively. The DVI-CDs-Cu were incubated at temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 80 °C for 1 h to study their thermal stability.

Concentration fixation of substrate: 150 mM H2O2 solution was prepared as substrate (30 mL) in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and different concentrations of DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme (0–20 μg mL−1) were added to it, while the oxygen concentration generated in the solution per minute was continuously monitored for 10 min by a dissolved oxygen meter. Concentration fixation of samples: H2O2 solutions with different concentrations were prepared as the substrate (30 mL) in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and 20 μg mL−1 DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme was added to it, while the oxygen concentration generated in the solution per minute during 10 min was continuously monitored by a dissolved oxygen meter. The CAT-like activity of DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme was directly revealed by the catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the DVI-CDs-Cu in cigarette filters
ce:section.ce:para: The cigarettes used in the real sample experiments were a brand from China Tobacco Industry Co., LTD. The acetate fiber from the original cigarette filter was removed, soaked in deionized water, and then combined with 50 mg of DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme. After thorough mixing and blending, the fiber was dried at 40 °C and subsequently loaded into a purchased commercial hollow filter (Fig. S1).

The filter's elimination efficiency was determined using a custom-made apparatus in which a cigarette was fixed into the filter, a rubber hose was connected to the mouth of the filter, two wash bottles with indicator solution were connected in the center, and a vacuum pump was connected to the end of the device to simulate smoking action. Each cigarette was burned for 15 min.

Evaluation of scavenging efficiency: PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) with NBT (0.5 mM) added as an indicator solution for •O2 – was loaded into a gas wash bottle. The indicator solution was exposed to smoke passing through the filter with or without the DVI-CDs-Cu from one burned cigarette. The absorbance of the indicator solution at 560 nm was measured and the elimination efficiency was calculated as Eq. (3).
(3)

Where A0 is the absorbance of the NBT, and A1 and A2 are the absorbances of reduced NBT at 560 nm without and with nanozyme filtration, respectively.

PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) with the addition of TA (5 mM) as an indicator solution for •OH was loaded into another gas wash bottle. TA reacts with •OH to become a fluorescent substance that emits fluorescence at a wavelength of 425 nm under 315 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity was measured before and after filtration, and the scavenging efficiency was calculated according to Eq. (4).
(4)

Where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of TA, and F1 and F2 are the fluorescence intensities at 425 nm in the absence and presence of nanozyme filtration, respectively.

Evaluation of recycling tests: After replacing the indicator solution in the wash bottle, the single filter was subjected directly to the next cigarette combustion test without any treatment, and the indicator solution after each cigarette combustion was collected to determine the above indexes and calculate the elimination efficiency according to Eq. (3) and (4).

The inhibitory activities of DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were determined by plate assay. Firstly, frozen strains of the two bacteria were taken out of the refrigerator at −80 °C and defrosted at room temperature, then cultured by inoculation into solid media and incubated for 16 h in an ultraclean bench. An individual colony was taken and incubated in LB medium overnight. The activated bacteria were washed out from the solid medium to make a bacterial solution, and the incubation was continued with monitoring of the OD values at 600 nm wavelength (OD600) to ensure the concentration was 108 CFU mL−1. Then, the bacterial solution was incubated with DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu groups at 37 °C and 180 rpm with shaking for 3 h, respectively. Equivalent DVI-CDs or DVI-CDs-Cu concentration in different groups was 20 μg mL−1. Finally, 100 μL of bacterial suspensions were applied to solid media and incubated overnight at 37 °C, and colony counts were determined.

The morphology and dimension size of DVI-CDs were observed and measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S2, the DVI-CDs exhibit good dispersion and uniform size distribution, with an average dimension size of 6.1 ± 0.3 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the DVI-CDs shows a distinct broad peak at 21°, characteristic of amorphous carbon (Fig. S3). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the DVI-CDs display an absorption peak at 280 nm due to the π-π* transition of the internal sp2 hybridized carbon and the n-π* transition of the surface C = O bonds and other groups (Fig. 1 a) (Huang et al., 2022). We also explored its photoluminescent properties. Most CDs emit fluorescence under UV excitation, however, the DVI-CDs lack the characteristic UV excitation-dependence. The fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the excitation wavelength at the same concentration with the emission peak at 520 nm falling within the green visible light range (Fig. S4), exhibiting a slight redshift (Fig. S5). This suggests that DVI-CDs are not suitable for applications such as fluorescence imaging. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to characterize the DVI-CDs and investigate the formation of functional groups on the surface. As shown in Fig. 1b, the FTIR results reveal major characteristic peaks at 3424, 2949, 1623, 1401, 1063, and 625 cm−1. The broad peak observed at 3424 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH/–NH, while the signal at 2949 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of –CH bonds. The double bond characteristic peak from C = C/C = O appears at a typical frequency of 1623 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2023), while the vibrations of C-N and C-O-C produce characteristic signals at 1401 and 1063 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 625 cm−1 and the four consecutive weak absorption peaks to its left originate from the bending vibrations of –OH and –CH2 (Qu et al., 2023). DVI-CDs with abundant functional groups have the potential to be complex with metal ions.

The TEM images reveal the formation of copper nanosheets by Cu2+ under the influence of functional groups on the surface of DVI-CDs, with the observable presence of CDs (Fig. 1c). Prolonged sonication leads to the appearance of holes on the surface of DVI-CDs-Cu (Fig. S6), indicating that DVI-CDs are attached to the surface of copper nanosheet skeleton rather than being encapsulated within it. XRD analysis demonstrates distinct diffraction peaks for the prepared DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme, which differ from those observed in XRD standard cards for Cu, CuO, Cu2O, and Cu(OH)2 (Fig. 1d). This distinctive crystalline structure suggests that during preparation, the NaOH used for pH adjustment did not result in combination and crystallization of copper oxide or copper hydroxide compounds, nor the reduction to metal crystals (Linet al., 2020; Xuet al., 2022). The FTIR result of DVI-CDs-Cu (Fig. 1b) reveals that the characteristic peaks associated with the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH functional groups and C = O binding undergo both splitting and shifting upon the introduction of Cu2+. A new peak attributed to the stretching vibrations of Cu-O/Cu-S appears at 484 cm−1 (Xu et al. 2023a, b). The absorption peak of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of DVI-CDs-Cu is observed to be blue-shifted in comparison to that of DVI-CDs, appearing at 259 nm with a discernible change in shape (Fig. 1a). These results collectively suggest the successful formation of chemical bonding between Cu2+ and DVI-CDs. This chemical bonding primarily involves the coordination complex formed through attractive interactions between Cu2+ and the hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups present on the surface of DVI-CDs, which can provide lone pair electrons.

As further evidence supporting the aforementioned conclusions, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to elucidate the elemental composition and coordination of DVI-CDs-Cu. The XPS full-scan spectrum of DVI-CDs-Cu demonstrates the presence of elements C, N, O, S, and Cu (Fig. S7 and Table S1), which is consistent with the raw materials utilized in this study. The relatively weak intensity of N elemental signals suggests a lower abundance of amino functional groups in the nanozyme, it is primarily –OH and C = O functional groups on the surface of CDs that predominantly contribute to the complexation and adsorption with Cu2+. Moreover, considering that the sole introduction of S element from an external source during preparation is through CuSO4, it can be inferred that after Cu2+ is adsorbed and coordinated by DVI-CDs, sulfur mainly exists as SO4 2- ions in the solution. Notably discernible signals persist even after repeated washing and centrifugation steps, indicating the presence of sulfur-containing functional groups on DVI-CDs' surface that play a significant role in coordinating complexes with Cu2+. Specifically, binding energies of 284.8, 286.3, and 288.4 eV can be observed on the C 1 s high-resolution spectrum of DVI-CDs-Cu, which are assigned to the chemical binding peaks of C-C/C = C/C-S (Llopiz and Maire, 1979), C-N/C-O (Uvdal et al., 1992), and C = O (Demri and Muster, 1995), respectively (Fig. 1e), and these signals are predominantly derived from the DVI-CDs. In the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p, which contain binding energy peaks arising from the binding of the respective elements to Cu2+: 398.8 eV (Cu-N) (Greulich et al., 2015), 531.5 eV (Cu-O) (Deroubaix and Marcus, 1992), and 168.5 eV (Cu-S) (Chen et al., 2021) (Fig. 1f-1 h). The high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p shows that the main and characteristic vibrational satellite peaks of Cu 2p3/2 appear at 935 and 942.5 eV, while the main and characteristic vibrational satellite peaks of Cu 2p1/2 appear at 955 and 962.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 1i). It is a typical feature of the XPS high-resolution spectrum belonging to Cu2+ (Nefedov et al., 1977), and the possibility of Cu2+ being reduced to Cu0 can be excluded.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to support the above ideas with assistance. Fig. S8 represents the molecular model simulations of DVI-CDs and is utilized to calculate the potential adsorption sites upon complexation with Cu2+. The electron cloud map obtained from these calculations visualizes the optimal potential distribution on the DVI-CDs (Fig. S9). The blue regions indicate electrostatically negative regions that have an affinity to cationic species, which concentrate near the N, O, and S atoms in their respective functional groups, and the detailed energy data are presented in Table 1 . Whether present in the carboxyl or carbonyl group, the O atom attached to the double bond exhibits a higher affinity for complexation with Cu2+. Although the absolute value of ΔG for adsorption complexation with –OH is relatively small, it still represents one of the potential sites for adsorption. All these results demonstrate the successful preparation of DVI-CDs-Cu composite nanozyme.

To investigate the SOD-like activity of DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu, the scavenging efficiency of DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu against •O2 – was evaluated using NBT as an indicator. It is a commonly used superoxide indicator, which can be reduced by •O2 – to a violet precipitate (Fig. S10), as reflected by the absorption peak at 560 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 a and d, in the blank control without adding samples, the absorbance of NBT at 560 nm increases continuously with •O2 –. However, after the addition of DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu, the absorbance of NBT at 560 nm decreases significantly over the same reaction period, and the degree of scavenging is greater with the increase of concentration (Fig. 2b and e). This suggests that both DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu exhibit SOD-like activity. The abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface binding •O2 – and electron transfer in the presence of carbonyl groups conjugated to the π system are responsible for the SOD-like activity of DVI-CDs (Gao et al. 2023a, b). In terms of the final scavenging effect, the DVI-CDs samples without Cu2+ complexation achieve up to 85 % scavenging efficiency of •O2 – (Fig. 2c), whereas the copper nanosheets adsorbed around the DVI-CDs catalytically decompose the products of the previous reaction after the disproportionation of •O2 – by the DVI-CDs, which improve the efficiency of the previous reaction, thus enhancing the overall scavenging of •O2 –, resulting in overall scavenging of •O2 – by the DVI-CDs-Cu scavenging efficiency of •O2 – is close to 100 % (Fig. 2f). After storage for some time at different temperature conditions, DVI-CDs-Cu still maintains robust activity, ensuring its subsequent application can be carried out successfully (Fig. S11).

We also investigated the scavenging efficiency of DVI-CDs and DVI-CDs-Cu for •OH using TMB as an indicator. TMB can be oxidized by •OH to form blue oxTMB, which has a distinct absorption peak at 652 nm (Fig. S12). A Fenton reaction based on Fe2+/H2O2 was used to generate •OH. Fig. 3 a and d show that the generation of •OH in the absence of sample addition leads to an increase in the absorption peak at 652 nm. However, the addition of DVI-CDs or DVI-CDs-Cu effectively inhibits the formation of oxTMB (Fig. 3b and e), directly reflected in the color change from blue to yellow (Fig. S13). Notably, DVI-CDs exhibit a more efficient •OH scavenging effect compared to DVI-CDs-Cu, scavenging approximately 95 % of •OH at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3c), while DVI-CDs-Cu achieves a scavenging efficiency of 78 % at the same concentration (Fig. 3f). This difference is attributed to the Fenton-type reaction of Cu2+, where the presence of copper nanosheets catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 in the substrate to •OH (Liu and Wang, 2023), expressing peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity (Lin et al., 2019). This suggests potential applications in chemodynamic therapy (CDT) (Zhang et al. 2023a, b), although it is not explored further in this work. Similarly, DVI-CDs-Cu remains effective in scavenging •OH even after incubation at different temperature conditions for a while (Fig. S14).

Since DVI-CDs-Cu exhibits POD-like activity and the ability to scavenge •OH, the TMB oxidative color reaction is no longer suitable for indicating its ability to catabolically disproportionate H2O2. Therefore, we evaluated the catalase-like (CAT-like) activity of DVI-CDs-Cu by quantifying the dissolved oxygen in the solution, which served as the final product (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the amount and rate of oxygen production within 10 min in a solution with 150 mM H2O2 as the substrate increase with the concentration of DVI-CDs-Cu, suggesting a positive correlation between the DVI-CDs-Cu content and CAT-like activity. This positive correlation is also observed with varying concentrations of the H2O2 substrate (Fig. 4c). When the concentration of DVI-CDs-Cu is fixed at 20 μg mL−1, higher concentrations of H2O2 lead to greater amounts and rates of oxygen production. During the dissolved oxygen determination, oxygen bubbles generated by DVI-CDs-Cu catalysis could be observed, and as the concentration of DVI-CDs-Cu increased, more bubbles were generated within the same time (10 min) (Figs. S15 and S16). Generally, the abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl functionalities provide complex adsorption and catalytic active sites, the nanosheet structure with a large specific surface area facilitates the exposure of more active sites, and the highly efficient electron transfer at the carbon–metal interface collectively contribute to the strong antioxidant function of DVI-CDs-Cu.

The effect of DVI-CDs-Cu on the elimination of •O2 – and •OH from the smoke produced by real cigarette samples was tested by loading DVI-CDs-Cu into the cigarette filters and simulating smoking using the device shown in Fig. S17. NBT and terephthalic acid (TA) were used as indicators for •O2 – and •OH, respectively. The results showed a significant reduction of •O2 – from cigarette smoke when using the DVI-CDs-Cu-loaded filter compared to the filter without it, with the optimal scavenging efficiency reaching 83.16 % (Fig. 4d). Additionally, the fluorescence probe TA, sensitive to •OH, was employed to assess the scavenging efficiency of •OH before and after filtration through the filter tip. The DVI-CDs-Cu filter achieved an optimal efficiency of 53.75 % in scavenging •OH from cigarette smoke (Fig. 4e). Continuous simulation experiments with multiple cigarettes demonstrated that the DVI-CDs-Cu filter maintains good stability in eliminating ROS from cigarette smoke, retaining 78.64 % and 45.37 % elimination of •O2 – and •OH respectively after filtering five cigarettes (Fig. 4f and g). However, a decreasing trend in scavenging efficiency was observed throughout the simulations. This decline may be attributed to substances such as cigarette tar adhering to the surface of DVI-CDs-Cu and the decomposition caused by persistent high-temperature burning, which reduces its catalytic activity. These results indicate that the DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme-loaded cigarette filter can efficiently eliminate ROS from cigarette smoke, presenting a broad application perspective.

Considering that cigarette smoke may carry microorganisms from the filtration system into the smoker, S. aureus (a Gram-positive bacteria) and E. coli (a Gram-negative bacteria) were selected as typical microbial contaminants for growth inhibition assays using the plate assay to evaluate the antibacterial performance of DVI-CDs-Cu. As shown in Fig. 5 a, DVI-CDs derived from the herbal components exhibit excellent antibacterial performance, with inhibition rates of 91.2 % and 90.9 % against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. The abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of DVI-CDs can cover the outer surface of the bacteria, isolating bacterial cells and preventing them from consuming nutrients and proliferating, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth (Yu et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2019). After complexing with copper ions, these ions can enter the interior of bacteria by binding with bacteria-specific enzymes to disrupt the bacterial membrane or directly kill the bacteria (Zhao et al., 2023), increasing the antibacterial rates of DVI-CDs-Cu to 96.3 % and 98.4 % against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Fig. 5b and c). The experimental results demonstrate that DVI-CDs-Cu is effective against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. DVI-CDs-Cu with both antioxidant and antibacterial properties is better than reported materials (Table S2).

In summary, we have developed a composite nanozyme of DVI-CDs-Cu with exceptional antioxidant and antimicrobial properties by utilizing DVI-CDs derived from three medicinal herbs: Dandelion, Violet, and Isatis root, which were complexed with Cu2+ ions. Benefiting from the abundant surface functional groups, the DVI-CDs exhibited excellent SOD-like activity while forming stable complexes with copper ions. The attached copper ions performed a POD-like function, creating a cascade effect that greatly enhanced the antioxidant capacity of the DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme and strengthened its antibacterial property. By effectively scavenging •O2 –, •OH, and H2O2, the potential application of the DVI-CDs-Cu nanozyme as a ROS scavenger was demonstrated in cigarette filtering systems. This provides a new example for the design and development of CDs-metal ions synergistic bionano-enzymes.
